ABEND.notes: The ABEND procedure deletes a process or process pair and signals that the deletion was caused by an abnormal condition

ACTIVATEPROCESS.notes: The ACTIVATEPROCESS procedure returns a process or process pair from the suspended state to the ready state

ADDDSTTRANSITION.notes: The ADDDSTTRANSITION procedure allows a super-group user (255, n ) to add an entry to the daylight- saving-time (DST) transition table

ADDRESS_DELIMIT.notes: The ADDRESS_DELIMIT[64]_ procedures obtain the addresses of the first and last bytes of a particular area of the caller's logical address space

ADDRTOPROCNAME.notes: The ADDRTOPROCNAME procedure accepts a P register value and stack marker ENV value and returns the associated symbolic procedure name and various optional items that describe the procedure in detail

ALLOCATESEGMENT.notes: The ALLOCATESEGMENT procedure allocates a selectable extended data segment for use by the calling process

ALTER.notes: The ALTER procedure changes certain attributes of a disk file that are normally set when the file is created

ALTERPRIORITY.notes: The ALTERPRIORITY procedure is used to change the execution priority of a process or process pair

ARMTRAP.notes: Registers trap handler address globally in OS.

params.name: indicates that an I/O operation completed but a warning occurred (call FILE_GETINFO_ or FILEINFO).

AWAITIO.notes: The AWAITIO, AWAITIOX, and AWAITIOXL procedures are used to complete a previously initiated I/ O operation

BACKSPACEEDIT.notes: The BACKSPACEEDIT procedure sets the current record number of an IOEdit file to that of the line preceding what was the current record before the call

BINSEM_CLOSE_.notes: The BINSEM_CLOSE_ procedure closes access to a binary semaphore

BINSEM_CREATE_.notes: The BINSEM_CREATE_ procedure creates, opens, and locks a binary semaphore

BINSEM_FORCELOCK_.notes: The BINSEM_FORCELOCK_ procedure forces a lock on a binary semaphore

BINSEM_GETSTATS_.notes: The BINSEM_GETSTATS_ procedure returns counter statistics for one or more binary semaphores for a specified process

BINSEM_ISMINE_.notes: The BINSEM_ISMINE_procedure allows a process or thread to query whether it is the current lock owner of the semaphore specified by the semID parameter

BINSEM_LOCK_.notes: The BINSEM_LOCK_ procedure locks a binary semaphore

BINSEM_OPEN_.notes: The BINSEM_OPEN_ procedure opens a binary semaphore

BINSEM_STAT_VERSION_.notes: The BINSEM_STAT_VERSION_ procedure accepts a version number defined in kbinsem.h for the BINSEM_GETSTATS_ procedure and checks whether or not it matches the implementation version of BINSEM_GETSTATS_

BINSEM_UNLOCK_.notes: The BINSEM_UNLOCK_ procedure unlocks a binary semaphore

BREAKMESSAGE_SEND_.notes: The BREAKMESSAGE_SEND_ procedure sends a break-on-device message to a specified process

CANCEL.notes: The CANCEL procedure is used to cancel the oldest incomplete operation on a file opened nowait

CANCELPROCESSTIMEOUT.notes: The CANCELPROCESSTIMEOUT procedure cancels a process-time timer previously initiated by a call to the SIGNALPROCESSTIMEOUT procedure

CANCELREQ.notes: The CANCELTIMEOUT procedure cancels an elapsed-time timer previously initiated by a call to the SIGNALTIMEOUT procedure

CHANGELIST.notes: The CHANGELIST procedure is used only when the application program acts as a supervisor or tributary station in a centralized multipoint configuration

CHECKALLOCATESEGMENT.notes: The CHECKALLOCATESEGMENT procedure allocates a selectable extended data segment for use by the backup process in a process pair

CHECKCLOSE.notes: The CHECKCLOSE procedure is called by a primary process to close a designated file in its backup process

CHECKDEALLOCATESEGMENT.notes: The CHECKDEALLOCATESEGMENT procedure deallocates an extended data segment from use by the backup process in a process pair

CHECKDEFINE.notes: The CHECKDEFINE procedure is used to update a backup process with a DEFINE that was changed in the primary process

CHECKMONITOR.notes: The CHECKMONITOR procedure is called by a backup process to monitor the state of the primary process and to return control to the appropriate point (in the backup process) in the event of a failure of the primary process

CHECKOPEN.notes: The CHECKOPEN procedure is called by a primary process to open a designated file for its backup process

CHECKPOINT.notes: The CHECKPOINT procedure is called by a primary process to send information about its current executing state to its backup process

CHECKPOINTMANY.notes: The CHECKPOINTMANY procedure (like the CHECKPOINT procedure) is called by a primary process to send information about its current executing state to its backup process

CHECKPOINTMANYX.notes: The CHECKPOINTMANYX procedure (like the CHECKPOINTX procedure) is called by a primary process to send information about its current executing state to its backup process

CHECKPOINTX.notes: The CHECKPOINTX procedure (like the CHECKPOINTMANYX procedure) is called by a primary process to send information about its current executing state to its backup process

CHECKRESIZESEGMENT.notes: The CHECKRESIZESEGMENT procedure complements the RESIZESEGMENT procedure

CHECKSETMODE.notes: The CHECKSETMODE procedure allows a primary process of a process pair to propagate SETMODE operations to the backup process of the pair

CHECKSWITCH.notes: The CHECKSWITCH procedure is called by a primary process to cause the duties of the primary and backup processes to be interchanged

CHECK^BREAK.notes: The CHECK^BREAK procedure tests whether the BREAK key has been typed since the last CHECK^BREAK

CHECK^FILE.notes: The CHECK^FILE procedure checks the file characteristics of a specified file

CHILD_LOST_.notes: The CHILD_LOST_ procedure examines a system message to determine whether it indicates that a specified process or process pair has been lost

CLOSE.notes: The CLOSE procedure closes an open file

CLOSEALLEDIT.notes: The CLOSEALLEDIT procedure closes all open IOEdit files

CLOSEEDIT.notes: The CLOSEEDIT procedure closes a specified file that was opened by the OPENEDIT or OPENEDIT_ procedure

CLOSEEDIT_.notes: The CLOSEEDIT_ procedure closes a specified file that was opened by the OPENEDIT or OPENEDIT_ procedure

CLOSE^FILE.notes: The CLOSE^FILE procedure closes a specified file

COMPLETEIOEDIT.notes: The COMPLETEIOEDIT procedure informs IOEdit that an outstanding I/O request has finished

COMPRESSEDIT.notes: The COMPRESSEDIT procedure copies a specified EDIT file to a new EDIT file that it creates

COMPUTEJULIANDAYNO.notes: The COMPUTEJULIANDAYNO procedure converts a Gregorian calendar date on or after January 1, 0001, to a Julian day number

COMPUTETIMESTAMP.notes: The COMPUTETIMESTAMP procedure converts a Gregorian (common civil calendar) date and time into a 64-bit Julian timestamp

CONFIG_GETINFO_BYNAME2_.notes: The CONFIG_GETINFO_BYLDEV2_ and CONFIG_GETINFO_BYNAME2_ procedures obtain the logical and physical attributes of a device

CONFIG_GETINFO_BYNAME_.notes: The CONFIG_GETINFO_BYLDEV_ and the CONFIG_GETINFO_BYNAME_ procedures obtain the logical and physical attributes of a device

CONTIME.notes: The CONTIME procedure converts a 48-bit timestamp to a date and time in integer form

CONTROL.notes: The CONTROL procedure is used to perform device-dependent I/O operations

CONTROLBUF.notes: The CONTROLBUF procedure is used to perform device-dependent I/O operations requiring a data buffer

CONTROLMESSAGESYSTEM.notes: The CONTROLMESSAGESYSTEM procedure controls the maximum number of receive and send messages used by a process

CONVERTASCIIEBCDIC.notes: The CONVERTASCIIEBCDIC procedure translates the 256 EBCDIC encodings to and from the 256 eight-bit ASCII encodings

CONVERTPROCESSNAME.notes: The CONVERTPROCESSNAME procedure converts a process name from local to network form

CONVERTPROCESSTIME.notes: The CONVERTPROCESSTIME procedure is used to convert the quad microsecond process time returned by the PROCESSTIME, MYPROCESSTIME, or PROCESSINFO procedure into hours, minutes, seconds, milliseconds, and microseconds

CONVERTTIMESTAMP.notes: The CONVERTTIMESTAMP procedure converts a Greenwich mean time (GMT) timestamp to or from a local-time-based timestamp within any accessible node in the network

CPUTIMES.notes: The CPUTIMES procedure returns the length of time (in microseconds) that a given processor has spent in these states since it was loaded or reloaded:  These times reflect the amount of time spent by the processor (from the last system load or reload) in a process environment, an interrupt environ...

CREATE.notes: The CREATE procedure is used to define a new structured or unstructured disk file

CREATEPROCESSNAME.notes: The CREATEPROCESSNAME procedure returns a unique process name suitable for passing to the NEWPROCESS and NEWPROCESSNOWAIT procedures

CREATEREMOTENAME.notes: The CREATEREMOTENAME procedure supplies a process name that is unique for the specified system in a network

CREATORACCESSID.notes: The CREATORACCESSID procedure is used to obtain the creator access ID (CAID) of the process that created the calling process

CRE_Arccos_Real64_.notes: mod                   Returns the remainder of an integer division calculation

CRE_Arcsin_Real64_.notes: The Arccos functions return the angle, expressed in radians, whose cosine is cos. The

CRE_Arctan2_Real64_.notes: The Arctan functions return the angle, expressed in radians, whose tangent is tan.

CRE_Cosh_Real64_.notes: is the angle, expressed in radians, who cosine is returned.

CRE_Exp_Real32_.notes: An arithmetic fault occurs if an intermediate calculation causes an overflow.

CRE_File_Close_.notes: The CRE_File_Close_ function closes the standard file that you specify.

CRE_File_Control_.notes: after decrementing its count by one, the number of outstanding connections is nonzero,

CRE_File_Input_.notes: For more information on the CONTROL system procedure, see the Guardian

CRE_File_Message_.notes: If file_ordinal does not reference a null file, CRE_File_Input_ reads the next

CRE_File_Open_.notes: INT PROC CRE_File_Open_( file_ordinal, flags, access,

CRE_Log_Message_.notes:     CRE_Hometerm_Open_ opens your process’s home terminal and returns its file

CRE_Mod_Int64_.notes: The Mod functions return the result of dividing their first parameter by their second

CRE_Power2_Real64_.notes: The Power functions cause a fault if:

CRE_Putenv_.notes: Functions, to obtain environment information such as the process startup message,

CRE_Sinh_Real64_.notes: is the angle, expressed in radians, whose sine is returned.

CRE_Sqrt_Real32_.notes: Split returns the fractional part of number.

CRE_Stacktrace_.notes: The syntax for the TNS environment is:

CRE_Strtod_.notes:     Zero if substr is not found within str.

CRE_StrtoulX_.notes: ! d gets 138051, the decimal equivalent of hex 21B43.

CRE_Strtoul_.notes: ! d gets 138051, the decimal equivalent of hex 21B43.

CRE_Tanh_Real32_.notes: is the angle, expressed in radians, whose tangent is returned.

CRE_Tanh_Real64_.notes: The Tan functions return the tangent of angle:

CRTPID_TO_PROCESSHANDLE_.notes: The CURRENTSPACE procedure returns the ENV register (as saved in the stack marker) and a string (in ASCII) containing the space ID of the caller

DAYOFWEEK.notes: The DAYOFWEEK procedure takes a 32-bit Julian day number and returns the corresponding day of the week

DEALLOCATESEGMENT.notes: The DEALLOCATESEGMENT procedure deallocates an extended data segment when it is no longer needed by the calling process

DEBUG.notes: The DEBUG procedure invokes the debugging facility on the calling process

DEBUGPROCESS.notes: The DEBUGPROCESS procedure invokes the debugging facility on a process

DEFINEADD.notes: The DEFINEADD procedure adds a DEFINE to the calling process' context using the attributes in the working set

DEFINEDELETE.notes: The DEFINEDELETE procedure allows the caller to delete a DEFINE from the calling process' context

DEFINEDELETEALL.notes: The DEFINEDELETEALL procedure allows the caller to delete all DEFINEs from the calling process' context

DEFINEINFO.notes: The DEFINEINFO procedure returns selected information about a DEFINE

DEFINELIST.notes: The DEFINELIST procedure is used only when the application process is acting as a supervisor or tributary station in a centralized multipoint configuration

DEFINEMODE.notes: The DEFINEMODE procedure allows the caller to control the use of DEFINEs (the DEFINE mode of the process)

DEFINENEXTNAME.notes: The DEFINENEXTNAME procedure returns the name of the DEFINE that follows the specified DEFINE (in ASCII order)

DEFINEPOOL.notes: The DEFINEPOOL procedure designates a portion of a user's stack, global data or a data segment for use as a pool

DEFINEREADATTR.notes: The DEFINEREADATTR procedure allows the caller to obtain the current value of an attribute in a DEFINE in the calling process' context or in the working set

DEFINERESTORE.notes: The DEFINERESTORE procedure uses a saved version of a DEFINE in the user's buffer to create an active DEFINE

DEFINERESTOREWORK.notes: The DEFINERESTOREWORK procedure restores the working set from the background set

DEFINESAVE.notes: The DEFINESAVE procedure copies an active DEFINE or the current working attribute set into a user buffer

DEFINESAVEWORK.notes: The DEFINESAVEWORK procedure saves the DEFINE working set in the background set

DEFINESETATTR.notes: The DEFINESETATTR procedure allows the caller to modify the value of an attribute in the working set

DEFINESETLIKE.notes: The DEFINESETLIKE procedure can be used to initialize the working set with the attributes in an existing DEFINE

DEFINEVALIDATEWORK.notes: The DEFINEVALIDATEWORK procedure can be used to check the working set for consistency

DELAY.notes: The DELAY procedure permits a process to suspend itself for a timed interval

DELETEEDIT.notes: The DELETEEDIT procedure deletes from an EDIT file all lines that have line numbers in a specified range

DEVICEINFO.notes: The DEVICEINFO procedure is used to obtain the device type and the physical record length of a file

DEVICEINFO2.notes: The DEVICEINFO2 procedure is used to obtain the device type and the physical record length of a file, which can be open or closed

DEVICE_GETINFOBYLDEV_.notes: The DEVICE_GETINFOBYLDEV_ procedure obtains the physical and logical attributes of a device

DEVICE_GETINFOBYNAME_.notes: The DEVICE_GETINFOBYNAME_ procedure obtains the physical and logical attributes of a device

DISKINFO.notes: The DISKINFO procedure obtains information about disk volumes

DISK_REFRESH_.notes: The DISK_REFRESH_ procedure causes control information to be written to the specified disk volume

DNUMIN.notes: The DNUMIN[64_] procedures convert the ASCII characters used to represent a number into the signed 32-bit integer value for that number

DNUMOUT.notes: The DNUMOUT procedure converts unsigned double word integer values to their ASCII equivalents

DST_GETINFO_.notes: The DST_TRANSITION_DELETE_ procedure allows a super-group user (255,n) to delete an existing entry from the daylight saving time (DST) transition table

DST_TRANSITION_MODIFY_.notes: The DST_TRANSITION_MODIFY_ procedure allows a super-group user (255,n) to modify an entry in the daylight-saving-time (DST) transition table

EDITREAD.notes: The EDITREAD procedure reads text lines from an EDIT file (file code=101)

EDITREADINIT.notes: The EDITREADINIT procedure is called to prepare a buffer in the application program's data area for subsequent calls to EDITREAD

ERRNO_GET_.notes: The ERRNO_GET_ procedure obtains the value of the errno variable set by many OSS, native C/C++, and some Guardian routines

EXTENDEDIT.notes: The EXTENDEDIT procedure copies an EDIT file to a new file that it creates and that has a larger extent size than the original file

FILEERROR.notes: The FILEERROR procedure is used to determine whether an I/O operation that completed with an error should be retried

FILEINFO.notes: The FILEINFO procedure obtains error and characteristic information about a file

FILEINQUIRE.notes: This procedure does not have a C syntax, because it is superseded and should not be used for new development

FILENAME_COMPARE_.notes: The FILENAME_COMPARE_ procedure compares two file names to determine whether they refer to the same object

FILENAME_DECOMPOSE_.notes: The FILENAME_DECOMPOSE_ procedure extracts and returns one or more parts of a file name or file-name pattern

FILENAME_EDIT_.notes: The FILENAME_EDIT_ procedure modifies one or more parts of a file name or file-name pattern, changing them to a specified value

FILENAME_FINDFINISH_.notes: The FILENAME_FINDFINISH_ procedure releases the resources reserved for a search that was previously initiated by a call to FILENAME_FINDSTART_

FILENAME_FINDNEXT.notes: The FILENAME_FINDNEXT_ procedure returns the next name in a set of named entities that was defined by a call to the FILENAME_FINDSTART_ procedure

FILENAME_FINDSTART_.notes: The FILENAME_FINDSTART_ procedure sets up a search of named entities

FILENAME_MATCH_.notes: The FILENAME_MATCH_ procedure determines whether one or more contiguous sections of a file name match the corresponding sections of a file-name pattern (that is, whether the file name sections might be represented by the pattern sections)

FILENAME_RESOLVE_.notes: The FILENAME_RESOLVE_ procedure converts a partially qualified file name to a fully qualified file name

FILENAME_SCAN_.notes: The FILENAME_SCAN_ procedure checks for valid file-name syntax and returns the length in bytes of that part of the input string that constitutes a file name

FILENAME_TO_OLDFILENAME_.notes: The FILENAME_TO_OLDFILENAME_ procedure converts a file name from external format to the legacy internal file-name format

FILENAME_TO_PATHNAME_.notes: The FILENAME_TO_PATHNAME_ procedure converts a Guardian file name or subvolume name to an OSS pathname

FILENAME_TO_PROCESSHANDLE_.notes: The FILENAME_TO_PROCESSHANDLE_ procedure converts a file name to a process handle

FILENAME_UNRESOLVE_.notes: The FILENAME_UNRESOLVE_ procedure accepts a file name as input, deletes left-hand sections that match the default values, and returns a file name that is semantically equivalent to the input file name

FILERECINFO.notes: The FILERECINFO procedure obtains record characteristics of a disk file

FILE_ALTERLIST_.notes: The FILE_ALTERLIST_ procedure changes certain attributes of a disk file that are normally set when the file is created

FILE_AWAITIO64.notes: The FILE_AWAITIO64[U]_ procedures are used to complete a previously initiated I/O operation

FILE_CLOSE_.notes: The FILE_CLOSE_ procedure closes an open file

FILE_CLOSE_CHKPT_.notes: The FILE_CLOSE_CHKPT_ procedure is called by a primary process to close a designated file in its backup process

FILE_COMPLETE_GETINFO_.notes: The FILE_COMPLETE_GETINFO_ procedure provides information about the set of files that are currently enabled for completion and thus can be completed by a FILE_COMPLETE..

FILE_COMPLETE_SET_.notes: The FILE_COMPLETE_SET_ procedure enables a set of Guardian and Open System Services (OSS) files for completion by subsequent calls to FILE_MCOMPLETE_ or a FILE_COMPLETE..

FILE_CONTROL64_.notes: The FILE_CONTROL64_ procedure is used to perform device-dependent I/O operations

FILE_CONTROLBUF64_.notes: The FILE_CONTROLBUF64_ procedure is used to perform device-dependent I/O operations requiring a data buffer

FILE_CREATELIST_.notes: The FILE_CREATELIST_ procedure is used to define a new structured or unstructured disk file

FILE_CREATE_.notes: The FILE_CREATE_ procedure is used to define a new structured or unstructured disk file

FILE_FDGETTYPE_.notes: The FILE_FDGETTYPE_ procedure accepts a file descriptor of the opened POSIX file as an input and returns the corresponding file type

FILE_GETINFOBYNAME_.notes: The FILE_GETINFOBYNAME_ procedure obtains a limited set of information about a file identified by file name

FILE_GETINFOLISTBYNAME_.notes: The FILE_GETINFOLISTBYNAME_ procedure obtains detailed information about a file identified by file name

FILE_GETINFOLIST_.notes: The FILE_GETINFOLIST_ procedure obtains detailed information about a file identified by file number

FILE_GETINFO_.notes: The FILE_GETINFO_ procedure obtains a limited set of information about a file identified by file number

FILE_GETLOCKINFO_.notes: The FILE_GETLOCKINFO_ procedure obtains information about locks (held or pending) on a local disk file or on a set of files on a local disk volume

FILE_GETOPENINFO_.notes: The FILE_GETOPENINFO_ procedure obtains information about the opens of one disk file or all the files on a disk device, or the opens of certain nondisk devices

FILE_GETRECEIVEINFO.notes: The FILE_GETRECEIVEINFO[L]_ procedures return information about the last message read on the $RECEIVE file

FILE_GETSYNCINFO_.notes: The FILE_GETSYNCINFO_ procedure is called by the primary process of a process pair before starting a series of write operations to a file open with paired access

FILE_LOCKFILE64_.notes: The FILE_LOCKFILE64_ procedure is used to exclude other users from accessing a file (and any records within that file)

FILE_LOCKREC64_.notes: The FILE_LOCKREC64_ procedure excludes other users from accessing a record at the current position

FILE_MCOMPLETE.notes: The FILE_MCOMPLETE_ API is similar to FILE_COMPLETE[64|L]_ APIs, with the added ability to complete one or more previously initiated I/O operations for a Guardian file or return ready information for one OSS file in a single call per the file complete set defined for the caller

FILE_OPEN_.notes: The FILE_OPEN_ procedure establishes a communication path between an application process and a file

FILE_OPEN_CHKPT_.notes: The FILE_OPEN_CHKPT_ procedure is called by a primary process to open a designated file for its backup process

FILE_READ64_.notes: The FILE_READ64_ procedure returns data from an open file to the application process' data area

FILE_READLOCK64_.notes: The FILE_READLOCK64_ procedure sequentially locks and reads records in a disk file, exactly like the combination of FILE_LOCKREC64_ and FILE_READ64_

FILE_READUPDATE64_.notes: The FILE_READUPDATE64_ procedure reads data from a disk or process file in anticipation of a subsequent write to the file

FILE_READUPDATELOCK64_.notes: The FILE_READUPDATELOCK64_ procedure is used for random processing of records in a disk file

FILE_RENAME_.notes: The FILE_RENAME_ procedure changes the name of an open disk file

FILE_REPLY64_.notes: The FILE_REPLY64_ procedure is used to send a reply message to a message received earlier in a corresponding call to READUPDATE[X|XL] or FILE_READUPDATE64_ on the $RECEIVE file

FILE_RESTOREPOSITION_.notes: The FILE_RESTOREPOSITION_ procedure supersedes the REPOSITION Procedure (Superseded by FILE_RESTOREPOSITION_ Procedure) and is used to position a disk file to a saved position (the positioning information having been saved by a call to the FILE_SAVEPOSITION_ Procedure

FILE_SAVEPOSITION_.notes: The FILE_SAVEPOSITION_ procedure supersedes the SAVEPOSITION Procedure (Superseded by FILE_SAVEPOSITION_ Procedure) , and is used to save a disk file's current file positioning information in anticipation of a need to return to that position

FILE_SETKEY_.notes: The FILE_SETKEY_ procedure supersedes the KEYPOSITION[X] Procedures

FILE_SETLASTERROR_.notes: The FILE_SETLASTERROR_ procedure is used to set the error information for a file identified by the file number

FILE_SETMODENOWAIT64_.notes: The FILE_SETMODENOWAIT64_ procedure is used to set device-dependent functions

FILE_SETPOSITION_.notes: The FILE_SETPOSITION_ procedure supersedes the POSITION Procedure

FILE_SETSYNCINFO_.notes: The FILE_SETSYNCINFO_ procedure is used by the backup process of a process pair after a failure of the primary process

FILE_UNLOCKFILE64_.notes: The FILE_UNLOCKFILE64_ procedure unlocks a disk file and any records in the file currently locked by the user

FILE_UNLOCKREC64_.notes: The FILE_UNLOCKREC64_ procedure unlocks a record currently locked by the user

FILE_WRITE64_.notes: The FILE_WRITE64_ procedure writes data from an array in the application program to an open file

FILE_WRITEREAD.notes: The FILE_WRITEREAD[64]_ procedures perform a writeread request to a file

FILE_WRITEUPDATE64_.notes: The FILE_WRITEUPDATE64_ procedure transfers data from an array in the application program to a file

FILE_WRITEUPDATEUNLOCK64_.notes: The FILE_WRITEUPDATEUNLOCK64_ procedure is used for random processing of records in a disk file

FIXSTRING.notes: The FIXSTRING procedure is used to edit a string based on subcommands provided in a template

FNAME32COLLAPSE.notes: The FNAME32COLLAPSE procedure converts the 32-character file name used by the Distributed Name Service to external format for display

FNAME32EXPAND.notes: The FNAME32EXPAND procedure expands a partial file name from the compacted external form to the 32-character file name used by the Distributed Name Service (DNS) programmatic interface

FNAME32TOFNAME.notes: The FNAME32TOFNAME procedure converts a file name from the 32-character format used by the Distributed Name Service (DNS) to its internal format

FNAMECOLLAPSE.notes: The FNAMECOLLAPSE procedure converts a file name from internal to external form

FNAMECOMPARE.notes: This procedure is supported for compatibility with previous software and should not be used for new development

FNAMEEXPAND.notes: The FNAMEEXPAND procedure is used to expand a partial file name from the compacted external form to the standard 12-word internal form usable by other file-system procedures

FNAMETOFNAME32.notes: The FNAMETOFNAME32 procedure converts a file name from the 12-word internal format to the 32- character Distributed Name Service (DNS) format

FORMATCONVERT.notes: The FORMATCONVERT and FORMATCONVERTX procedures convert a format (a data record layout described by means of edit descriptors) from external form to the internal form that is required for presentation to the FORMATDATA[X] procedures

FORMATDATA.notes: The FORMATDATA (which is superseded by FORMATDATAX) and FORMATDATAX procedures convert data item values between internal and external representations, as specified by a format (previously converted from external to internal form by FORMATCONVERT[X]) or by the list-directed conversion rules

FP_IEEE_DENORM_GET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_DENORM_GET_ procedure reads the IEEE floating-point denormalization mode

FP_IEEE_DENORM_SET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_DENORM_SET_ procedure sets the IEEE floating-point denormalization mode

FP_IEEE_ENABLES_GET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ENABLES_GET_ procedure reads the IEEE floating-point trap enable mask

FP_IEEE_ENABLES_SET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ENABLES_SET_ procedure sets the IEEE floating-point trap enable mask

FP_IEEE_ENV_CLEAR_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ENV_CLEAR_ procedure sets the floating-point environment (consisting of the rounding mode, the exception flags, the trap enables, and the denormalization mode) back to its initial values

FP_IEEE_ENV_RESUME_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ENV_RESUME_ procedure restores the floating-point environment (the rounding mode, the exception flags, the trap enables, and the denormalization mode) to the values it had before calling FP_IEEE_ENV_CLEAR_

FP_IEEE_EXCEPTIONS_GET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_EXCEPTIONS_GET_ procedure reads the IEEE floating-point exception mask

FP_IEEE_EXCEPTIONS_SET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_EXCEPTIONS_SET_ procedure sets the IEEE floating-point exception mask

FP_IEEE_ROUND_GET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ROUND_GET_ procedure reads the current rounding mode

FP_IEEE_ROUND_SET_.notes: The FP_IEEE_ROUND_SET_ procedure sets the current rounding mode

GETCPCBINFO.notes: The GETCPCBINFO procedure provides a process with information from its own (the current) process control block (PCB)

GETCRTPID.notes: The GETCRTPID procedure is used to obtain the four-word CRTPID (which contains the process name or creation timestamp in words [0:2] and cpu,pin in word [3]) associated with a process

GETDEVNAME.notes: The GETDEVNAME procedure obtains the name associated with a logical device number

GETINCREMENTEDIT.notes: The GETINCREMENTEDIT procedure returns the record number increment value for an IOEdit file

GETPOOL.notes: The GETPOOL procedure obtains a block of memory from a buffer pool initialized by the DEFINEPOOL procedure

GETPOOL_PAGE_.notes: The GETPOOL_PAGE_ procedure obtains a block of memory from a buffer pool

GETPOSITIONEDIT.notes: The GETPOSITIONEDIT procedure returns the record number (1000 times the EDIT line number) of the line in the specified file most recently read or written (that is, it returns the current record number)

GETPPDENTRY.notes: The GETPPDENTRY procedure is used to obtain a description of a named process pair by its index into the destination control table (DCT)

GETREMOTECRTPID.notes: The GETREMOTECRTPID procedure is used to obtain the four-word process ID associated with a remote process

GETSYNCINFO.notes: The GETSYNCINFO procedure is called by the primary process of a process pair before starting a series of write operations to a file open with paired access

GETSYSTEMNAME.notes: The GETSYSTEMSERIALNUMBER procedure returns the system serial number of the caller's system as an ASCII character string of numerals

GIVE^BREAK.notes: The GIVE^BREAK procedure returns BREAK to the previous owner (the process that owned BREAK before the last call to TAKE^BREAK)

GROUPIDTOGROUPNAME.notes: The GROUPIDTOGROUPNAME procedure returns the group name associated with an existing group ID from the USERID file

GROUPMEMBER_GETNEXT_.notes: The GROUPMEMBER_GETNEXT_ procedure returns a user member or alias associated with a group ID

GROUPNAMETOGROUPID.notes: The GROUPNAMETOGROUPID procedure returns the group ID associated with an existing group name from the USERID file

GROUP_GETINFO_.notes: The GROUP_GETINFO_ procedure returns attributes of the specified group, such as the group's textual description and whether the group is automatically deleted when the last member is deleted

GROUP_GETNEXT_.notes: The GROUP_GETNEXT_ procedure returns a group name and group ID

HALTPOLL.notes: The HALTPOLL procedure is normally used to stop continuous polling

HEADROOM_ENSURE_.notes: The HEADROOM_ENSURE_ procedure allows you to check that the current stack has enough room for the needs of your process

HEAPSORT.notes: The HEAPSORT procedure is used to sort an array of equal-sized elements in place

HEAPSORTX_.notes: The HEAPSORTX_ procedure is used to sort an array of equal-sized elements in place

HIST_FORMAT_.notes: The HIST_FORMAT_ procedure produces an ASCII text representation of the process state whose context is established by a previous call to the HIST_INIT_ procedure or HIST_GETPRIOR_ procedure

HIST_GETPRIOR_.notes: The HIST_GETPRIOR_ procedure establishes a previous procedure call as the process context for display by the next HIST_FORMAT_ procedure call

HIST_INIT_.notes: The HIST_INIT_ procedure initializes a process history display or stack trace

INCREMENTEDIT.notes: The INCREMENTEDIT procedure sets the increment to be added to successive line numbers for lines that will be added to an EDIT file without explicitly specified line numbers

INITIALIZEEDIT.notes: The INITIALIZEEDIT procedure allocates the EDIT file segment (EFS) to be used by IOEdit and initializes the data structures that it contains

params.name: length of a message is 1028 bytes (including the trailing null characters).
is the length, in bytes, of the message.

INITIALIZER.notes: The INITIALIZER procedure is used to read the startup message and, optionally, to request receipt of assign and param messages sent by the starting process (which is often a TACL process)

INTERPRETINTERVAL.notes: The INTERPRETINTERVAL procedure takes a fixed variable (quad) containing a value representing a number of microseconds and converts it into a combination of days, hours, minutes, seconds, milliseconds, and microseconds

INTERPRETJULIANDAYNO.notes: The INTERPRETJULIANDAYNO procedure converts a Julian day number to the year, month, and day

INTERPRETTIMESTAMP.notes: The INTERPRETTIMESTAMP procedure converts a 64-bit Julian timestamp into a Gregorian (the common civil calendar) date and time of day

IPUAFFINITY_CONTROL_.notes: The IPUAFFINITY_CONTROL_ procedure controls scheduling features which are not associated with a single process

IPUAFFINITY_GET_.notes: The IPUAFFINITY_GET_ procedure returns the current IPU number associated with a process or KLT thread, which is the IPU number set through IPUAFFINITY_SET_, if that is in effect, or the current assignment by the process scheduler otherwise

IPUAFFINITY_SET_.notes: The IPUAFFINITY_SET_ procedure associates a process or KLT thread with a given IPU within its CPU

JULIANTIMESTAMP.notes: The JULIANTIMESTAMP procedure returns either a four-word, microsecond-resolution, Julian-date-based timestamp or the number of microseconds elapsed since the last system load

KEYPOSITION.notes: The KEYPOSITION[X] procedures are used to position by primary or alternate key within a structured file

LABELEDTAPESUPPORT.notes: The LABELEDTAPESUPPORT procedure is callable; it provides a way for nonprivileged programs to determine if tape label processing is enabled in the system

LASTADDR.notes: The LASTADDR procedure returns the ‘G'[0] relative address of the last word in the application process' data area

LASTADDRX.notes: The LASTADDRX procedure allows user programs to check stack limits or parameter addresses

LASTRECEIVE.notes: The LASTRECEIVE procedure is used to obtain the four-word process ID and the message tag associated with the last message read from the $RECEIVE file

LOCATESYSTEM.notes: The LOCATESYSTEM procedure provides the system number corresponding to a system name and returns the logical device number of the line handler controlling the path to a given system

LOCKFILE.notes: The LOCKFILE procedure is used to exclude other users from accessing a file (and any records within that file)

LOCKINFO.notes: The LOCKINFO provides information about locks (held or pending) on a local DP2 disk volume

LOCKREC.notes: The LOCKREC procedure excludes other users from accessing a record at the current position

LONGJMP_.notes: The LONGJMP_ procedure performs a nonlocal goto

LOOKUPPROCESSNAME.notes: The LOOKUPPROCESSNAME procedure is used to obtain a description of a named process pair by its name or by its index into the local destination control table (DCT)

MBCS_ANY_KATAKANA_.notes: The MBCS_ANY_KATAKANA_ procedure checks a string of HP Kanji characters for any Katakana characters

MBCS_CHARSIZE_.notes: The MBCS_CHARSIZE_ procedure returns the display size (in columns) and the storage size (in bytes) of multibyte character set (MBCS) characters from the character set specified by the charset parameter

MBCS_CHARSTRING_.notes: The MBCS_CHARSTRING_ procedure tests the contents of a data string for the exclusive use of MBCS characters of known internal character sets

MBCS_CHAR_.notes: The MBCS_CHAR_ procedure indicates whether a string of bytes is part of a Hewlett Packard Enterprise multibyte character set (MBCS) and that testmbcschar points to the first byte of a valid character of charset MBCS

MBCS_CODESETS_SUPPORTED_.notes: The MBCS_CODESETS_SUPPORTED_ procedure returns a 32-bit integer value

MBCS_DEFAULTCHARSET_.notes: The MBCS_DEFAULTCHARSET_ procedure returns the default multibyte character set (MBCS) identification

MBCS_EXTERNAL_TO_TANDEM_.notes: The MBCS_EXTERNAL_TO_TANDEM_ procedure translates a text string from a specified external format to the Hewlett Packard Enterprise internal text format

MBCS_FORMAT_CRT_FIELD_.notes: The MBCS_FORMAT_CRT_FIELD_ procedure formats Kanji only or mixed data types for specific terminal types

MBCS_FORMAT_ITI_BUFFER_.notes: The MBCS_FORMAT_ITI_BUFFER_ procedure formats ITI buffers for specific terminal types

MBCS_MB_TO_SB_.notes: The MBCS_MB_TO_SB_ procedure and the companion MBCS_SB_TO_MB_ procedure allow conversion of the ninety-four displayable characters of the ASCII character set between one-byte ASCII and characters of the specified MBCS

MBCS_REPLACEBLANK_.notes: The MBCS_REPLACEBLANK_ procedure replaces nonstandard blanks

MBCS_SB_TO_MB_.notes: The MBCS_SB_TO_MB_ procedure and the companion MBCS_MB_TO_SB_ procedure are provided to allow conversion of the ninety-four displayable characters of the ASCII character set between one-byte ASCII and characters of the specified MBCS

MBCS_SHIFTSTRING_.notes: The MBCS_SHIFTSTRING_ procedure upshifts or downshifts all alphabetic characters in a multibyte character set (MBCS) string

MBCS_TANDEM_TO_EXTERNAL_.notes: The MBCS_TANDEM_TO_EXTERNAL_ procedure translates a text string from Hewlett Packard Enterprise internal format to a specified external text format

MBCS_TESTBYTE_.notes: The MBCS_TESTBYTE_ procedure returns the identification of a specified byte contained within a text string of mixed data

MBCS_TRIMFRAGMENT_.notes: The MBCS_TRIMFRAGMENT_ procedure detects and trims trailing multibyte character fragments from text strings

MESSAGESTATUS.notes: The MESSAGESTATUS procedure determines if a particular message received through READUPDATE has been canceled

MESSAGESYSTEMINFO.notes: The MESSAGESYSTEMINFO procedure measures the current number of messages to or from a process so it can issue a warning when a limit is nearly reached

MOM.notes: The MOM procedure provides a process with the four-word process ID of its creator

MONITORCPUS.notes: The MONITORCPUS procedure instructs the operating system to notify the application process if a designated processor module either:  command)  The calling application process is notified by means of a system message read through the $RECEIVE file

MONITORNET.notes: The MONITORNET procedure enables or disables receipt of system messages concerning the status of processors in remote systems

MONITORNEW.notes: The MONITORNEW procedure enables or disables receipt of the SETTIME and Power On messages

MOVEX.notes: The MOVEX procedure moves data between extended data segments without the need for absolute addressing; it serves both privileged and nonprivileged users

MYGMOM.notes: The MYGMOM procedure provides a process that is a member of a batch job with the process ID of its job ancestor (GMOM)

MYPID.notes: The MYPID procedure provides a process with its own processor and PIN number

MYPROCESSTIME.notes: The MYPROCESSTIME procedure returns the process execution time of the calling process or KLT thread

MYSYSTEMNUMBER.notes: The MYSYSTEMNUMBER procedure provides a process with its own system number

NEWPROCESS.notes: The NEWPROCESS[NOWAIT] procedures are used to create a new process and, optionally, set a number of process attributes

NEXTFILENAME.notes: The NEXTFILENAME procedure is used to obtain the name of the next disk file on a designated volume

NODENAME_TO_NODENUMBER_.notes: The NODENAME_TO_NODENUMBER_ procedure converts a node name (system name) to the corresponding node number (system number)

NODENUMBER_TO_NODENAME_.notes: The NODENUMBER_TO_NODENAME_ procedure converts a node number (system number) to the corresponding node name (system name)

NODE_GETCOLDLOADINFO_.notes: The NODE_GETCOLDLOADINFO_ procedure retrieves the name of the OSIMAGE file from which the specified node was system loaded

NO^ERROR.notes: The NO^ERROR procedure is called internally by sequential I/O (SIO) procedures

NSK_TIMER_GRANULARITY_.notes: The NSK_TIMER_GRANULARITY_ procedure reports the minimum granularity for processes using ordinary or fine timer granularity

NUMBEREDIT.notes: The NUMBEREDIT procedure renumbers the lines of an EDIT file that are in a specified range

NUMIN.notes: The NUMIN procedure converts the ASCII characters used to represent a number into the signed integer value for that number

NUMOUT.notes: The NUMOUT procedure converts unsigned integer values to their ASCII equivalents

OBJFILE_GETINFOLIST_.notes: The OBJFILE_GETINFOLIST_ procedure obtains information about the object file or user library file of the calling process

OLDFILENAME_TO_FILENAME_.notes: The OLDFILENAME_TO_FILENAME_ procedure converts a file name from legacy internal-file format to external format

OLDSYSMSG_TO_NEWSYSMSG_.notes: The OLDSYMSG_TO_NEWSYSMSG_ procedure converts a legacy-format system message to its default- format equivalent

OPEN.notes: The OPEN procedure establishes a communication path between an application process and a file

OPENEDIT.notes: The OPENEDIT procedure allocates and initializes data blocks in the EDIT file segment (EFS) so that the specified file can be accessed later by the IOEdit procedures

OPENEDIT_.notes: The OPENEDIT_ procedure allocates and initializes data blocks in the EDIT file segment (EFS) so that the specified file can be accessed later by the IOEdit procedures

OPENER_LOST_.notes: The OPENER_LOST_ procedure examines a system message and searches an open table to determine if an opener has been lost

OPENINFO.notes: The OPENINFO procedure obtains information about the opens of one disk file or of all the files on a disk device, or of certain nondisk devices

OPEN^FILE.notes: The OPEN^FILE procedure permits access to a file when using sequential I/O (SIO) procedures

OSS_PID_NULL_.notes: The OSS_PID_NULL_ procedure returns a null OSS process ID

PACKEDIT_.notes: The PACKEDIT procedure converts a line image from unpacked format into EDIT packed line format

PATHNAME_TO_FILENAME_.notes: The PATHNAME_TO_FILENAME_ procedure converts an OSS pathname to a Guardian file name

POOL64_AUGMENT_.notes: The POOL64_AUGMENT_ procedure adds a secondary memory segment to an existing pool

POOL64_CHECK_.notes: The POOL64_CHECK_ procedure checks the internal pool data structures and returns error information about the pool

POOL64_CHECK_SHRINK_.notes: The POOL64_CHECK_SHRINK_ procedure provides information for how to reduce the footprint of the pool

POOL64_DEFINE_.notes: The POOL64_DEFINE_ procedure designates an allocated 64-bit memory address range to use as the primary segment of a pool

POOL64_DIMINISH_.notes: The POOL64_DIMINISH_ procedure releases an empty secondary segment (if one exists) from an existing pool

POOL64_GETINFO_.notes: The POOL64_GETINFO_ procedure returns information about the specified pool

POOL64_GET_.notes: The POOL64_GET_ procedure allocates an element from the specified pool

POOL64_PUT_.notes: The POOL64_PUT_ procedure frees (returns to the designated pool) an element previously allocated by POOL64_GET_

POOL64_REALLOC_.notes: The POOL64_REALLOC_ procedure changes the size of the block of memory pointed to by the element parameter to the number of bytes specified by the newSize parameter and returns a pointer to the block of memory

POOL64_RESET_MAX_.notes: The POOL64_RESET_MAX_ procedure resets the max_… accumulators in the designated pool to the current value of the measured quantity

POOL64_RESIZE_.notes: The POOL64_RESIZE_ procedure shrinks or expands the size of a single-segment pool within the same address space

POOL_CHECK_.notes: The POOL_CHECK_ procedure checks the internal pool data structures and returns error information

POOL_DEFINE.notes: The POOL_DEFINE_ procedure designates a portion of a user's stack, global data, or a data segment for use as a pool

POOL_GETINFO.notes: The POOL_GETINFO_ procedure returns information about the specified pool

POOL_GETSPACE_.notes: The POOL_GETSPACE_ procedure obtains a block of memory from a buffer pool

POOL_GETSPACE_PAGE_.notes: The POOL_GETSPACE_PAGE_ procedure obtains a block of memory from a buffer pool

POOL_PUTSPACE.notes: The POOL_PUTSPACE_ procedure returns a block of memory to a buffer pool

POOL_RESIZE.notes: The POOL_RESIZE procedure changes the size of a pool that was initialized by the POOL_DEFINE procedure

POSITIONEDIT.notes: The POSITIONEDIT procedure sets the next record number to a specified value for a specified file

PRIORITY.notes: The PRIORITY procedure enables a process to examine or change its initial priority

PROCESSACCESSID.notes: The PROCESSACCESSID procedure is used to obtain the process access ID (PAID) of the calling process

PROCESSFILESECURITY.notes: The PROCESSFILESECURITY procedure is used to examine or set the file security for the current process

PROCESSHANDLE_COMPARE_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_COMPARE_ procedure compares two process handles and reports whether they are identical, represent different processes of the same process pair, or different

PROCESSHANDLE_DECOMPOSE_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_DECOMPOSE_ procedure returns one or more parts of a process handle

PROCESSHANDLE_GETMAIN_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_GETMAIN_ procedure obtains the process handle of the caller’s main thread in a KLT process

PROCESSHANDLE_GETMINE_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_GETMINE_ procedure obtains the caller's process handle

PROCESSHANDLE_NULLIT_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_NULLIT_ procedure initializes a process handle to a null value

PROCESSHANDLE_TO_CRTPID_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_TO_CRTPID_ procedure converts a process handle to the corresponding process ID (CRTPID)

PROCESSHANDLE_TO_FILENAME_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_TO_FILENAME_ procedure converts a process handle to a process file name

PROCESSHANDLE_TO_STRING_.notes: The PROCESSHANDLE_TO_STRING_ procedure converts a process handle to the equivalent process string

PROCESSINFO.notes: The PROCESSINFO procedure is used to obtain process status information

PROCESSNAME_CREATE_.notes: The PROCESSNAME_CREATE_ procedure returns a unique process name that is suitable for passing to the PROCESS_LAUNCH_, PROCESS_CREATE_, or PROCESS_SPAWN_ procedure

PROCESSORSTATUS.notes: The PROCESSORSTATUS procedure returns the highest processor number plus 1 of the configured processor modules in a system and the operational states of all the processor modules

PROCESSORTYPE.notes: The PROCESSORTYPE procedure returns the processor type of a specified system and processor

PROCESSOR_GETINFOLIST_.notes: The PROCESSOR_GETINFOLIST_ procedure obtains configuration information and statistics about a processor

PROCESSOR_GETNAME_.notes: The PROCESSOR_GETNAME_ procedure returns a processor's type and model

PROCESSSTRING_SCAN_.notes: The PROCESSSTRING_SCAN_ procedure scans an input string for a process string and returns the corresponding process handle or a single component of the process string converted to internal form

PROCESSTIME.notes: The PROCESSTIME procedure returns the process execution time of any process in the network

params.name: If this parameter is omitted, 0 is used.

PROCESS_ACTIVATE_.notes: The PROCESS_ACTIVATE_ procedure returns a suspended process or process pair to the ready state

PROCESS_CREATE_.notes: The PROCESS_CREATE_ procedure creates a new process and, optionally, assigns a number of process attributes

PROCESS_DEBUG_.notes: The PROCESS_DEBUG_ procedure invokes the debugging facility on the calling process or on another process

PROCESS_GETINFOLIST_.notes: The PROCESS_GETINFOLIST_ procedure obtains detailed information about a specified process or about a set of processes that meet specified criteria

PROCESS_GETINFO_.notes: The PROCESS_GETINFO_ procedure obtains a limited set of information about a specified process

PROCESS_GETPAIRINFO_.notes: The PROCESS_GETPAIRINFO_ procedure obtains basic information about a named process or process pair

PROCESS_LAUNCH_.notes: The PROCESS_LAUNCH_ procedure creates a new process and, optionally, assigns a number of process attributes

PROCESS_MONITOR_CPUS_.notes: The PROCESS_MONITOR_CPUS_ procedure instructs the operating system to notify the application process if a designated processor module either:  command)  The calling application process is notified by means of a system message read through the $RECEIVE file

PROCESS_MONITOR_NET_.notes: The PROCESS_MONITOR_NET_ procedure enables or disables receipt of system messages concerning the status of processors in remote systems

PROCESS_MONITOR_NEW_.notes: The PROCESS_MONITOR_NEW_ procedure enables or disables receipt of the SETTIME and Power On messages

PROCESS_MONITOR_VCORE_.notes: The PROCESS_MONITOR_VCORE_ procedure enables or disables the receipt of the Core Enablement system message (-149) on the $RECEIVE of the calling process, when any of he following conditions occur:  The number of enabled IPUs or CPUs changes when a new Core License is enabled which changes the lic...

PROCESS_SETINFO_.notes: The PROCESS_SETINFO_ procedure alters a single nonstring attribute of a specified process and optionally returns the prior value of the attribute

PROCESS_SETSTRINGINFO_.notes: The PROCESS_SETSTRINGINFO_ procedure alters a single string-form attribute of a specified process, and optionally returns the prior value of the attribute

PROCESS_SPAWN.notes: The PROCESS_SPAWN[64]_ procedure creates a new Open System Services (OSS) process and, optionally, assigns a number of process attributes

PROCESS_STOP_.notes: The PROCESS_STOP_ procedure deletes a process or process pair

PROCESS_SUSPEND_.notes: The PROCESS_SUSPEND_ procedure places a process or process pair into the suspended state, preventing that process from being active (that is, from executing instructions)

PROCESS_TIMER_OPTION_.notes: The PROCESS_TIMER_OPTION_ procedure queries or sets the process timer granularity in the calling process

PROCESS_TIMER_OPTION_PIN_.notes: The PROCESS_TIMER_OPTION_PIN_ procedure queries or sets the process timer granularity in a process specified by Process Identification Number (PIN)

PROGRAMFILENAME.notes: The PROGRAMFILENAME procedure is used to obtain the name of the calling process' program file

PURGE.notes: The PURGE procedure is used to delete a disk file that is not open

PUTPOOL.notes: The PUTPOOL procedure returns a block of memory to a buffer pool initialized by the DEFINEPOOL procedure

RAISE.notes: The RAISE_ procedure is the pTAL procedure name for the C raise() function

READ.notes: The READ[X] procedures return data from an open file to the application process' data area.The READ procedure is intended for use with 16-bit addresses, while the READX procedure is intended for use with 32-bitextended addresses

READEDIT.notes: The READEDIT procedure reads one line from a specified EDIT file and returns it to the caller in unpacked format

READEDITP.notes: The READEDITP procedure reads one line from a specified EDIT file and returns it to the caller in EDIT packed line format READEDITP is an IOEdit procedure and can only be used with files that have been opened by OPENEDIT or OPENEDIT_

READLOCK.notes: The READLOCK[X] procedures sequentially lock and read records in a disk file, exactly like the combination of LOCKREC and READ[X]

READUPDATE.notes: The READUPDATE[X|XL] procedures read data from a disk or process file in anticipation of a subsequent write to the file

READUPDATELOCK.notes: The RECEIVEINFO procedure is used to obtain the four-word process ID, message tag, error recovery (sync ID), and request-related (file number, read count, and I/O type) information associated with the last message read from the $RECEIVE file

READ^FILE.notes: The READ^FILE procedure is used to read a file sequentially

REFPARAM_BOUNDSCHECK.notes: This procedure is supported for compatibility with previous software and should not be used for new development

REMOTEPROCESSORSTATUS.notes: The REMOTEPROCESSORSTATUS procedure supplies the status of processor modules in a particular system in a network

REMOTETOSVERSION.notes: The RENAME procedure is used to change the name of a disk file that is open

REPLY.notes: The REPOSITION procedure is used to position a disk file to a saved position (the positioning information having been saved by a call to the SAVEPOSITION procedure)

RESETSYNC.notes: The RESETSYNC procedure is used by the backup process of a process pair after a failure of the primary process

RESIZEPOOL.notes: The RESIZEPOOL procedure changes the size of a pool that was initialized by the DEFINEPOOL procedure

RESIZESEGMENT.notes: The SAVEPOSITION procedure is used to save a disk file's current file positioning information in anticipation of a need to return to that position

RTL_Atoi_.notes: strncpy               Copies characters from one string to another.

RTL_Tan_Real32_.notes: An arithmetic fault occurs if number is less than zero.

RTL_Tan_Real64_.notes: An arithmetic fault occurs if number is less than zero.

RTL_Truncate_Real32_.notes: The Tanh functions return the hyperbolic tangent of number. The value returned is:

RTL_Truncate_Real64_.notes: The Tanh functions return the hyperbolic tangent of number. The value returned is:

RTL_Upper_Real64_.notes: The Truncate functions return the number whose absolute value is the largest integer

SEGMENTSIZE.notes: The SEGMENTSIZE procedure returns the size of the specified segment in bytes

SEGMENT_ALLOCATE.notes: The SEGMENT_ALLOCATE[64]_ procedures allocate data segments for use by the calling process

SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_CHKPT_.notes: The SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_CHKPT_ procedure is called by a primary process to allocate an extended data segment for use by its backup process

SEGMENT_DEALLOCATE_.notes: The SEGMENT_DEALLOCATE_ procedure deallocates an extended data segment

SEGMENT_DEALLOCATE_CHKPT_.notes: The SEGMENT_DEALLOCATE_CHKPT_ procedure is called by a primary process to deallocate an extended data segment in its backup process

SEGMENT_GETBACKUPINFO_.notes: The SEGMENT_GETBACKUPINFO_ procedure retrieves information about an extended segment that is allocated by the backup process in a named process pair

SEGMENT_GETINFO.notes: The SEGMENT_GETINFO[64]_ procedures retrieve information about currently allocated data segments

params.name: refers to the segment ID of the target segment or, if -1, indicates to iterate over all segments.

SEGMENT_GETINFOSTRUCT_.notes: The SEGMENT_GETINFOSTRUCT_ procedure retrieves information about a currently allocated data segment

SEGMENT_GET_MIN_ALIGN_.notes: The SEGMENT_GET_MIN_ALIGN_ procedure computes the minimum permissible alignment for a 64-bit segment of a given size

SEGMENT_GET_PREF_ALIGN_.notes: The SEGMENT_GET_PREF_ALIGN_ procedure computes the preferred alignment for a segment of a given size

SEGMENT_RESIZE64_.notes: The SEGMENT_RESIZE64_ procedure alters the size of an existing data segment (for example, a segment created by SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_ or SEGMENT_ALLOCATE64_)

SEGMENT_USE_.notes: The SEGMENT_USE_ procedure selects a particular extended data segment to be currently addressable by the calling process

SENDBREAKMESSAGE.notes: The SENDBREAKMESSAGE procedure allows user processes to send break-on-device messages to other processes

SETJMP_.notes: The SETJMP_procedure saves process context in a jump buffer

SETLOOPTIMER.notes: The SETLOOPTIMER procedure sets the caller's "process loop-timer" value

SETMODE.notes: The SETMODE procedure is used to set device-dependent functions

SETMODENOWAIT.notes: The SETMODENOWAIT procedure is used to set device-dependent functions in a nowait manner on nowait files

SETMYTERM.notes: The SETMYTERM procedure permits a process to change the terminal that is used as its home terminal (the default home terminal is the home terminal of a process' creator)

SETPARAM.notes: The SETPARAM procedure is used to set and fetch various values such as the station characteristics of network addresses

SETSTOP.notes: The SETSTOP procedure permits a process or KLT thread to protect itself from getting deleted by any process other than itself or its creator

SETSYNCINFO.notes: The SETSYNCINFO procedure is used by the backup process of a process pair after a failure of the primary process

SETSYSTEMCLOCK.notes: The SETSYSTEMCLOCK procedure allows you to change the system clock if you are a member of the super group

SET^FILE.notes: The SET^FILE procedure alters file characteristics and checks the old values of those characteristics being altered

SHIFTSTRING.notes: The SHIFTSTRING procedure upshifts or downshifts all alphabetic characters in a string

SIGACTION_INIT_.notes: The SIGACTION_INIT_ procedure establishes the caller's initial state of signal handling if default handling is not desired

SIGACTION_RESTORE_.notes: The SIGACTION_RESTORE_ procedure restores the signal-handling state saved by a previous call to the SIGACTION_SUPPLANT_ procedure

SIGACTION_SUPPLANT_.notes: This procedure can be called only from native processes

SIGJMP_MASKSET_.notes: The SIGJMP_MASKSET_ procedure saves a signal mask in a jump buffer that has already been initialized by the SIGSETJMP_ procedure

SIGNALPROCESSTIMEOUT.notes: The SIGNALPROCESSTIMEOUT procedure sets a timer based on process execution time, as measured by the processor clock

SIGNALTIMEOUT.notes: The SIGNALTIMEOUT procedure sets a timer to a given number of units of elapsed time, as measured by the processor clock

SIGSETJMP_.notes: The SIGSETJMP_ procedure saves process context in a jump buffer

SSIDTOTEXT.notes: The SSIDTOTEXT procedure converts the internal format subsystem ID to the external representation

STACK_ALLOCATE_.notes: The STACK_ALLOCATE_ procedure allocates a user stack segment for use as a thread or alternate signal stack

STACK_DEALLOCATE_.notes: The STACK_DEALLOCATE_ procedure releases memory resources for a user stack that was allocated using the STACK_ALLOCATE_ procedure

STEPMOM.notes: This procedure is supported for compatibility with previous software and should not be used for new development

STOP.notes: The STOP procedure deletes a process or process pair and indicates that the deletion was caused by a normal condition

SUSPENDPROCESS.notes: The SUSPENDPROCESS procedure puts a process or process pair into the suspended state, preventing that process from being active (that is, executing instructions)

SUTVER_GET_.notes: The SUTVER_GET_ procedure obtains information about the version of software on a system

SYSTEMCLOCK_SET_.notes: The SYSTEMCLOCK_SET_ procedure allows you to adjust or set the system clock if you are a member of the super group

SYSTEMENTRYPOINTLABEL.notes: The SYSTEMENTRYPOINTLABEL procedure returns either the procedure label of the named entry point or, if not found, a zero

SYSTEMENTRYPOINT_RISC_.notes: The SYSTEMENTRYPOINT_RISC_ procedure, which is defined only for native processes, returns either the 32-bit RISC address of the named entry point or, if not found, the value zero

TAKE^BREAK.notes: The TAKE^BREAK procedure enables BREAK monitoring for a file

TEXTTOSSID.notes: The TEXTTOSSID procedure scans a character string, expecting to find the external representation of a subsystem ID starting in the first byte (no leading spaces accepted)

TIME.notes: The TIME procedure provides the current date and time in integer form

TIMER_START_.notes: The TIMER_START_ procedure sets a timer to a given number of units of elapsed time, as measured by the processor clock

TIMER_STOP_.notes: The TIMER_STOP_ procedure stops a timer that was started using the TIMER_START_ procedure

TIMESTAMP.notes: The TIMESTAMP procedure provides the 48-bit form of the system clock where the application is running

TOSVERSION.notes: The TOSVERSION procedure identifies the version of the operating system that is running

TS_NANOSECS_.notes: The TS_NANOSECS_ procedure returns a value that represents the time (in nanoseconds) since the last coldload

TS_UNIQUE_COMPARE_.notes: The TS_UNIQUE_COMPARE_ procedure compares two unique timestamps created using the TS_UNIQUE_CREATE_ Procedure and returns a value indicating their relationship

TS_UNIQUE_CONVERT_TO_JULIAN_.notes: The TS_UNIQUE_CONVERT_TO_JULIAN_ procedure converts a unique timestamp into a Julian timestamp

TS_UNIQUE_CREATE_.notes: The TS_UNIQUE_CREATE_ procedure returns a 128-bit timestamp that is unique to the system it is generated on and any system in the same EXPAND network

UNLOCKFILE.notes: The UNLOCKFILE procedure unlocks a disk file and any records in that file currently locked by the user

UNLOCKREC.notes: The UNLOCKREC procedure unlocks a record currently locked by the user

UNPACKEDIT.notes: The UNPACKEDIT procedure converts a line image from EDIT packed line format into unpacked format

USERDEFAULTS.notes: The USERDEFAULTS procedure returns the default attributes of the specified user, such as the user's default volume/subvolume and default file security

USEREVENT.notes: The USEREVENTFILE_REGISTER_/ USEREVENT64_FILE_REGISTER_ procedure is used to establish a file number to access an application-defined user event set for an application that intends to use user events in combination with the file 32-bit or 64-bit tag I/O completion procedures

USERIDTOUSERNAME.notes: The USERIDTOUSERNAME procedure returns the user name, from the file $SYSTEM.SYSTEM.USERID, that is associated with a designated user ID

USERIOBUFFER_ALLOW_.notes: The USERIOBUFFER_ALLOW_ procedure dynamically sets the process behavior to be the same as if the objectfile flag "allow_user_buffers" were set in eld, provided it is called before opening any files

USERNAMETOUSERID.notes: The USERNAMETOUSERID procedure returns the user ID, from the file $SYSTEM.SYSTEM.USERID, that is associated with a designated user name

USER_AUTHENTICATE_.notes: The USER_AUTHENTICATE_ procedure verifies that a user exists and optionally logs on the user

USER_GETINFO_.notes: The USER_GETINFO_ procedure returns the default attributes of the specified user

USER_GETNEXT_.notes: The USER_GETNEXT_ procedure returns the next user name or alias in the order in which it is stored by the security mechanism in effect

USESEGMENT.notes: The USESEGMENT procedure selects a particular extended data segment to be currently addressable by the calling process

VERIFYUSER.notes: The VERIFYUSER procedure has three different functions

VRO_SET_.notes: The VRO_SET_ procedure causes a voluntary rendezvous opportunity to take place

WAIT^FILE.notes: The WAIT^FILE procedure is used to wait or check for the completion of an outstanding I/O operation

WRITE.notes: The WRITE[X] procedures write data from an array in the application program to an open file (see Considerations)

WRITEEDIT.notes: The WRITEEDIT procedure accepts a line in unpacked format, converts it into EDIT packed line format, and writes it to the specified file

WRITEEDITP.notes: The WRITEEDITP procedure accepts a line in EDIT packed line format and writes it to the specified file

WRITEEDIT_.notes: Writes a record to an edit file.

WRITEREAD.notes: The WRITEREAD[X] procedures write data to a file from an array in the application process, then wait for data to be transferred back from the file

WRITEUPDATE.notes: The WRITEUPDATE[X] procedures transfer data from an array in the application program to a file

WRITEUPDATEUNLOCK.notes: The WRITEUPDATEUNLOCK[X] procedures perform random processing of records in a disk file

WRITE^FILE.notes: The WRITE^FILE procedure writes a file sequentially

XBNDSTEST.notes: The XBNDSTEST procedure aids user programs in checking stack limits or parameter addresses

XSTACKTEST.notes: The XSTACKTEST procedure, used with LASTADDRX and XBNDSTEST procedures, checks stack limits
